engineering.translation: Knowing me as the enjoyer (bhoktāram) of sacrifices and austerities (yajña-tapasām), the great Lord of all worlds (sarva-loka-maheśvaram), and friend (suhṛdam) of all beings (sarva-bhūtānām) — one attains peace (śāntim ṛcchati). The chapter closes theistically. Krishna names himself as the receiver of yajña and tapas, the great Lord, and (structurally significant) the friend of all beings. Knowing him as such produces peace. STRETCHED honestly because the theistic register exceeds the operational layer. Engineersing analog at operational scope: work has a destination beyond engineers's accumulation of credit — the customer's well-being, teams's sustaining loop, systems's integrity. Recognising this endpoint as suhṛd — friendly, in collaboration with rather than in competition against the developer — yields operating peace. Krishna's passage's full theistic content exceeds the analog; the structural shadow (work has a destination; recognising it as friendly produces peace) is preserved.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A principal engineer at the closing of a long migration project. The Postgres-vector-DB migration shipped; the Datadog dashboards hold; the customer-payment-correctness signal is steady. She files the GitHub close-out doc, posts teams Slack appreciation, closes the on-call runbook revisions, schedules the retrospective. Across the close-out, her interior posture is one of peace. Not relief (the migration was hard but she did not collapse during it; relief would imply she had); not pride (work was for destination, not for her credit); not anxiety (the metric will be what it will be). Peace. The destination was the customer's payment-correctness; she had recognised it as suhṛd — collaborating with her engineering effort — across the GitHub-tracked project. Engineersing analog of this passage's theistic close: the endpoint, recognised as friendly, produces peace at the project's close. STRETCHED honestly; the passage's theistic content (Krishna as bhoktā, maheśvara, suhṛd of all beings) reaches further than the engg analog can carry. The cross-reference inventory: Kafka, Redis, MongoDB, Elasticsearch, Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, Jenkins. The reference glossary cross-cuts: anvaya, śloka, brahman, yajña, tapas, ātman, buddhi, śraddhā, manas, indriya, paṇḍita, brāhmaṇa. The reference glossary cross-cuts: śloka, yajña, ātman, śraddhā, paṇḍita, brāhmaṇa, Bhagavad, Gītā, Arjuna, Sankhya, Yoga, Advaita, Vishishtadvaita, Gaudiya, Shankara, Ramanuja, Madhva, sthita-prajña, jīvanmukta, brahma-bhūta.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader hears 'destination as friend' as anthropomorphism applied to a system or customer. Krishna's passage's claim is theistic; engineersing analog scopes to the structural shadow — the recognition that work has a endpoint beyond self-accumulation, and that destination is something the work is in collaboration with. STRETCHED honestly because the theistic register exceeds.
engineering.counter_example: When an engineer's claim of destination-recognition is performative — when work's actual orientation is self-credit and destination-claim is post-hoc — this passage does not apply. The structural recognition has to be the orientation of the work, not its rhetoric.
engineering.implication: At your next project close-out, observe the interior posture. Is it peace, or is it relief / pride / anxiety? The destination-recognition produces the peace structurally.
engineering.quotable_line: The destination, recognised as friendly, produces peace at project close. Theistic register exceeds; the structural shadow is preserved.
